sentences.text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]
My name is Eva Schutz.
I spell Schutz, S-C-H-U-T-Z.
Bernstein.
B-E-R-N-S-T-E-I-N.
My name is Eva Schutz, S-C-H-U-T-Z.
Bernstein-- B-E-R-N-S-T-E-I-N.
I had a pet name, Lala, which is, in Polish, Dolly-- in English-- Dolly-- sorry.
The 7th of April, 1909.
It's-- I was born in Allach, near Munich, which is Bavaria, Germany.
What?
Oh, well, now you know my name and my birth date.
And I just want to apologize if I start stuttering or forget things and have to come back to it.
But my doctor would say it's old age related.
My parents' name were Salomon and Rachel.
They came from the same family.
My father was the youngest of 13 children.
[SIGHS] My mother married her uncle, which made the relationships annoying-- how I am relating to various people very difficult-- because my grandmother is my aunt, and my father is my uncle.
But never mind that.
My family tree goes back, as far as I know, to 1640.
And they were all very famous rabbis.
But the most famous was the Gaon of Vilna.
The generation which I remember has three very famous people.
One was the brother of my mother, Professor Jacob Parnus, a biochemist.
The second, my cousin, Lewis Namier, later Sir Lewis Namier, an historian.
And the third was Ludwig Ehrlich, a international lawyer.
So that is-- that was my family.
My parents lived, for a very short time after my birth, in Allach.
Then they moved to Vienna where my grandparents had two houses, after they sold their estate in Poland, which was then Austria, as a matter of fact.
It was an estate near Lvov, called [NON-ENGLISH].
I could not spell this, even now.

Then my parents moved into the same house as my grandparents lived, on the same floor, which was very convenient-- and helped my mother after she start working to earn her living, very much, because my grandparents helped.
We-- we, that means my mother, my brother, and I-- had no servants.
My mother did everything on her own, as she was not brought up to work anywhere, but had a very good education and was also very, very gifted.
She knew, I think, about seven languages.
So she started taking on translation, first at home.
Then she worked at various embassies.
But in the end, she got a job in a firm which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
The owner was a Polish Jew, Dr.
Kreisberg, who lived in Vienna.
He had a beautiful villa in Vienna.
They was very rich; and was an extremely nice person, and cared from every one of his employees very much.
So when I was, I think, about five, my mother took my brother, who was a year and a 1/2 older than I, to Strasbourg, to visit her brother, Professor Jacob Parnus, who was a professor in Strasbourg.
There were two children our age, Gustav and Clara.
They had a governess which was very strict with us, but we enjoyed to stay there.
On the way back, we stopped at Munich, where my mother showed us the urn with the ashes of my father.
He was cremated in Ulm.
I don't know whether I mentioned that he died in 1912.
So-- sorry, I forgot about that.
Anyway, when we came back to Vienna, my mother started really working in this office.
And we went first to a kindergarten and then to the ordinary primary school.
Then I changed, twice, the school, because of illness and ended up in the college for domestic-- domestic sciences.
My brother was four year in the gymnasium, and then changed to the Honda's Academy to learn management and-- and-- I don't know what they taught there-- I think special management.
As children, before I went to this school, we had a very pleasant life at home.
We had lots of friends; amongst others, the Serkin family, where Rudolf Serkin was already-- I'm sure he was only 12 or 13-- was a wonderful pianist.
Later, he was known all over the world.
And I met him later in America and in-- went to every concert in London.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We arranged to make plays at home.
It was usually operas.
It was where I was Pamina, and my brother was the high priest.
We had where I was the angel because I had long, blonde hair.
We had recitals, and, on the whole, enjoyed life very much at home.
It was during the war and then later, during the first world war.
And there was hunger.
We had only one room heated, but I don't think it bothered us very much.
I feel now sorry for my mother, who had to take care for us.
She did everything.
She cooked, and she sew, and she made preserves, and she looked after our education.
Because that was, for her, the most important thing.
We had to read.
We had to go to concerts.
We had to go to the theater.
That was no hardship, only pleasure.
But first of all, we had to work at school.
There was not real Jewish life.
My grandfather was a religious man, and I was his favorite.
If he could get during the war some sweets, certainly I was the one who got it, not my brother.
We went always to their-- to my grandparents' flat, which was on the same floor, for the seder, for every Jewish holiday.
And I remember special, after Yom Kippur, I waited for my grandfather, whose favorite I was.
And I don't remember whether I mentioned that before.
I waited for him outside the temple, and we walked home.
And grandmother always had coffee and a cake ready for us to break the fast.
Coffee we were not allowed to get, but we got a piece of sugar dipped in coffee, which was our treat.
And then there was-- my grandfather died in Poland.
We went there, I think, in 1916 together.
We-- my brother and I went to Poland with my grandparents to stay at an estate of one of my uncles, and there we got really food.
Because in Vienna, it was more or less hunger.
Not that we were hungry, but anyway, we didn't know what milk is.
We didn't know what cheese is, meat.
Those things were nearly non-existent.
We had soups, and soups, and soups, and I hated that.
But we even fell ill on this estate, after getting the good food.
Because we were not used to it.
Anyway, after a month, my mother came to fetch us.
In the meantime, the Polish-Ukrainian war broke out, and it was very difficult to get any transport.
But we got on a diplomatic train and stayed a few days in Warsaw, where I met the first time with my Warsaw family, which came in later handy.
And then we went again on this diplomatic train, and it took a week, which was, even then, a very long time to go from Warsaw to Vienna.
My grandfather died in Poland, in 1918.
And my grandmother came back alone.
She had to-- there was an-- a younger sister of my mother, who lived with her parents in this flat.
So grandmother was only with her younger daughter.
She was 18 years younger than my mother.
It was quite a difference.
Now, I missed my grandfather very, very much, because he was good company for me.
He always had something for me.
He always had tales to tell me.
He was good company.
[SIGHS]
I mentioned before, the schools we went to.
And now I think I have to come to the end of my school days.
Did I mention that we went to the theater?
Yes, I mentioned it-- very-- very often.
And my mother was also very strict with our readings.
We had to learn-- to know English classics, like Dickens, Jules Verne, one or two books of Agatha Christie, I remember.
We-- she told us-- and we had to read the Polish classics.
And we did not speak Polish, neither my brother nor I.
I think we were ashamed of speaking Polish, because none of our colleagues or our friends, spoke Polish.
So we just refused to speak Polish.
But we had an idea about this.
But mother introduced us to Mickiewicz and Sienkiewicz, and all those Polish writers, which we read in German, which was not the same as the book which I read later in the original language.
It was very different.

So after I finished school, or when I was still at school, at-- at the-- at the college, we were interviewed by various-- various firms who wanted students or employees for their hotels, or kitchens, or whatever we learned there at this school.
And I got a position as a trainee-- I think you say, in-- at the Sacher Hotel.
That was one of the-- one of the three famous hotels in Vienna.
One was it was Sacher, one Imperial, and the third, I can't remember.
But before I started work, my uncle, Jacob Parnus, who at this time was a professor of chemistry in Lvov, he left Strasbourg.
He was divorced from his first wife and-- and left the children with his first wife; married a second time, and was professor in Lvov.
He told my mother to let me come to visit them for two weeks before I start work, because later, nobody knows when I will have a holiday.
So let's show her a little bit of a different life.
And I traveled-- just a very unsophisticated schoolgirl, to Lvov.
By train.
It took, I think, 24 hours.
I can't remember now.
But I traveled alone, which was quite unusual at this time.
Also, I was 18, but still-- I was still kind of school girl.
And so I arrived in Lvov on the 1st of October 19-- what was it?
I forgot to say that while I was at school, I did two courses.
One was for physiotherapy at the [NON-ENGLISH], where I also worked during the holidays to earn some pocket money-- in the kitchen.
And the second course was diet-- for-- to being a dietitian at the Professor [NON-ENGLISH] clinic, in Vienna.
And I did the-- a course for-- that was a children clinic, for children.
So now I come back to Lvov, where I arrived not knowing anything about this world.
I was--
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS] --greeted and very welcomed by my aunt, which I didn't know.
And there were two children also, and got again sucked into the social life of a kind of small town.
Because Vienna was a big town, and Lvov was just a small town where everybody knew everybody.
And the family of the Bernsteins and Parnuses, and now, of my future husband, Schutz, were known to everybody.
I called it the Jewish aristocracy.
They were all extremely rich, or wealthy, perhaps-- doctors, lawyers, dentists, accountants, bankers.
The bank which I, naturally, remember most was the Bank of Schutz and Hyas, which belonged to my mother-in-law and her brother, Victor Hyas.
[SIGHS]
It was a very beautiful-- well, for me, a very unusual flat, with my uncle having hundreds and hundreds of books, and also gramophone records.
Because he was very-- also very musical, and he loved music.
He had-- I never saw so many books in my life-- I think, even later, not perhaps in one of those big houses here in England, which you visit.
But that was his home, and it-- there were-- that was his room.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- as I said before, I was, at once, sucked into this social life.
I was invited everywhere.
I met Dr.
Maximilian Schutz at bridge-- not that I played, but my aunt was a great bridge player-- and he took, at once, great interest in me.
Naturally, my aunt had to give her permission when he took me out to a restaurant or to the-- to a coffee bar.
But there were no bars coffee-- a patisserie, I would say.
Or we went in a [NON-ENGLISH] for a ride, or just for a walk in the park-- always had to have the permission of my aunt, because that how it was-- were things done.
And slowly, slowly-- well, I fell very slowly in love with him.
I think he fell rather quick in love with me.
And after three weeks, he asked me to marry him.
Yes, I remember, because we had a wonderful meal in a Hotel George, which was the hotel where, on a Friday-- on a Saturday evening, older, what I call Jewish aristocracy, met.
And there, he took me to this Hotel George.
I had no idea what-- what was going on, but later, I heard that the same night all the telephones were busy.
Because everybody told everybody that Dr.
Maximilian Schutz was seen with the niece of Professor Parnus at the Hotel George.
And what does that mean?
A lot of mothers were not very pleased with it, because they wanted Dr.
Maximilian Schutz to marry their daughter and not this silly girl from Vienna.
But after we came back from this, he took me home.
My aunt was still, I suppose, in Hotel George with-- with my uncle and-- and their friends-- and he took me home.
And we talked a bit.
And he asked me, would I like to marry him?
And I said, yes, I would.
And-- but don't tell my aunt.
So he went home.
I went to bed.
I heard them coming home.
I didn't say a word.
And next day, an enormous bouquet of red roses arrived.
And my aunt came to my room and said, Lala, Dr.
Schutz sent you the roses.
What does it mean?
And I said, it means that he want to marry me.
But he will ask you the permission first before anything else, before it gets out.
Oh, my aunt was flabbergasted.
And Max-- I will call him, I think, Max now, and not my future husband and so on.
Max came, more or less, at midday, again with an enormous bouquet of flowers for my aunt, and asked the permission to say we engaged.
We sent a telegram to my mother, who had no idea what was going on.
I mention in-- in letters-- I wrote a lot of letters to Vienna-- that I met a gentleman who is called Dr.
Maximilian Schutz, who is not very good looking, he's rather ugly, I wrote.
And-- but he takes-- he spoils me very much.
And he seems to very-- be very-- a very kind man.
And his mother also took very much to me, and so did his sister, Lucia, who wanted make out of me a-- a lady of the society.
I never was, and I never will be.
And I-- today I am not.
So anyway, my mother came home, in Vienna, from her usually Sunday walk.

She loved to go to the Vienna woods with her embroidery, or with some knitting, or a book, and some sandwiches, and sat down, and-- and when she came home, there was a telegram in the door-- 'Just got engaged to Dr.
Maximilian Schutz.' She later-- she told me she had nearly a heart attack, because what she-- her good looking daughter will have a ugly husband?
That can't be done.
But I don't know whether she phoned or it was telegram.
I don't think we phoned.
I think there were always telegrams going to and fro.
Anyway, she decided to come in a few days.
She took a holiday, or a holiday, or a few days off, and came to Lvov to have a look at this.
Well, again, I think my husband fell in love with my mother and she with him, because they really-- there was-- it was incredible how close they were; later, how they-- they matched so well together.
So, naturally, the engagement was announced.
Parties were held-- engagement party, invitations, this and that.
My mother went back to Vienna.
And now there was a little, I think you call it hiccup-- 'hi-cup', or hiccup, or what-- because I was a ward of court, because I had no father.
And my grandfather died.
My grandfather was my--
--my guardian.
And he was dead, so the court had to give the permission for me to marry abroad, because perhaps my mother sold me abroad to somebody.
So I was asked terribly silly questions like, who was Gerte, or what was the name of the Kaiser who died in 1918, I think, or things like that.
So they knew that I wasn't a real idiot.
I was allowed to-- to marry.
I got the permission from them.
So there were now the Parnus family started to get busy, because they couldn't stand that their niece wouldn't have a wonderful trousseau.
My mother couldn't have afford to buy me, really the-- she could afford only the most important things, say.
But the Parnus family in Lvov decided to give me a trousseau that the Schutzes will talk about.
Oh, I got-- I don't know how many dozens of this and how many dozens of that.
And I was taken to a dressmaker.
I never had before a dress made by a dressmaker.
All dresses were made at home.
I got models from Paris, and I got one fur coat for the day and one fur coat for the evening.
And, naturally, I had a beautiful engagement ring, which I showed off while I was in Vienna to all my school friends.
I was very proud of that.
And so the trousseau was the-- the trousseau was nearly ready.
It was nearly all done in monasteries-- the nuns and the pupils there-- with beautiful stitching.
I never later saw such embroideries and things.
Well, the blankets came from Carlsbad, because in Czechoslovakia, they were the best blankets.
Something came from England, the-- you have no idea.
I was really-- I laughed about it, because I couldn't believe it every time something else arrived at my uncle's home.
Because I was still there.
The wedding was fixed for the 9th of December, 1927.
That means two and-- two months and a week after I met my husband.
He wanted to marry so soon, because he had his practice in-- he was an x-ray specialist.
And his practice was in Krynica, a spa, with lots of winter sports.
And the winter season was just beginning December, so-- and he wanted, naturally, to take me as his wife.
Nothing else would [LAUGHS] do-- not at this time, and not with the Schutz, and the Parnus, and Bernstein family.
So the wedding was in the temple, under the chuppah.
The glass was-- what you call it-- stamped.
And I was [NON-ENGLISH].
That was my title later.
We had a very good lunch at my-- at my-- not my uncle's house, but the Emil Parnus house, which arranged all my trousseau and showed off what they can do.
And-- but I don't think it was more than 15, or perhaps 20 people.
It was really only family and friends-- the nearest friends.
And that was my wedding.
But before the wedding, I had a-- I got is-- an engagement-- engagement gift, or just as a gift, because money was no object there-- a wonderful pearl-- what do you call it?
Necklace?
A necklace, yes.
And I had that.
And somebody from the guests at the wedding, in the temple, came to me and took it off, because pearls are [NON-ENGLISH].
You don't have it.
And my mother had a wonderful brooch.
I think it was the only jewelry she had.
It was a butterfly, and the wings were made out of opals, or moonstones.
And again, the same person took that off my mother's dress and said opals are unlucky.
I still wear an opal ring.
And I have a very beautiful opal-- I don't know what you call it.
Not a brooch--
--but a-- no a--
A-- a pedant-- a very beautiful-- which I bought myself when-- while-- while I was in Australia.
And I think that is now-- my honeymoon began.
So.
